elements.name: Description
texts: Bobby expresses his confusion at the extravagant embodied devotion and extreme austerity of the pilgrimage to Lough Derg and that it exposes a side of Jenny that he has never seen before.

elements.name: Source
texts: The Lovers of the Lake', in The Collected Stories of Sean O'Faolain, Vol. 2, pp. 18-43, here p. 22-23

elements.name: Rights
texts: Citation for the purposes of criticism

elements.name: Text
texts: “’Poor Jenny! I wish you’d talked like this to me before. It is, after all, as you say, your own affair. But what I can’t get over is that this thing you’re doing is so utterly extravagant. To go off to an island, in the middle of a lake, in the mountains, with a lot of Crawthumpers of every age and sex, and no sex, and peel off your stockings and your shoes, and go limping about on your bare feet on a lot of sharp stones, and kneel in the mud, psalming and beating your breast like a criminal, and drink nothing for three days but salt water…it’s not like you. It’s a side of you I’ve never known before. The only possible explanation for it must be that something is happening in you that I’ve never seen happen before.’”
